Design a detailed implementation plan for a School Stack landing page. This is a high-fidelity marketing page — the user wants a masterclass in UI/UX, no vibe-coded look, professional, built to convert school business owners.

## Project Context

- **Stack**: Next.js 16.2.4, React 19.2.4, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS 4.2.2
- **Location**: /Users/juliuscecilia/Desktop/schoolstack/schoolstack_landing/
- **Current state**: Blank slate — only src/app/page.tsx (empty), src/app/globals.css (@import "tailwindcss"), src/app/layout.tsx (bare bones)
- **Framer-motion**: NOT installed yet. Must be installed.
- **Next.js 16 key change**: All request APIs are async (params, cookies, headers). For a static marketing page this doesn't matter much since we have no dynamic routes.
- **Tailwind v4**: No tailwind.config.js. Configuration done via `@theme` in globals.css. Utility classes map from theme tokens:
  - `--color-{name}` → `bg-{name}`, `text-{name}`, `border-{name}`
  - `--radius-{name}` → `rounded-{name}`
  - `--shadow-{name}` → `shadow-{name}`
  - `--font-{name}` → `font-{name}`

## Design Spec Summary

**Design tokens (colors)**:
```css
--bg: #f6f5f2; --bg-alt: #f1efe9; --surface: #ffffff; --surface-soft: #fbfaf7; --surface-muted: #f3f1ec;
--border: #e7e3dc; --border-strong: #d9d4cc;
--text: #1f1e1a; --text-muted: #6f6b65; --text-faint: #9a958d;
--accent: #244b46; --accent-hover: #1d3d39; --accent-soft: #dfe8e5;
```

**Fonts**: Instrument Serif (display) + Inter (body/UI) via next/font/google

**Page sections** (in order):
1. Navbar — transparent → surface on scroll past 40px, logo left + nav links center + CTA right, mobile drawer
2. HeroSection — founder-led editorial split, text left (45%), product visual right (55%), eyebrow "Built inside a microschool", H1: "We built the software we couldn't find to run our own school."
3. PainSection — centered narrow column, H2 "Most microschools are too big for spreadsheets and too small for enterprise software.", pain tool strip (Google Forms, Venmo, Gmail, etc.)
4. FounderStoryBridgeSection — 2-col asymmetric, left story text, right collage of product crop + founder note card + school facts card
5. ProductPreviewSection — 7-tab interactive product preview (Website, Enrollment, Admin [default], Parents, Teachers, Payments, Leads) with placeholder HTML/CSS mockups for each tab
6. WorkflowSection — eyebrow "How it works", H2 "From founder chaos to a school that runs cleanly in three steps.", 3 step cards with connecting line
7. StacksSection — 3 tier cards (Starter $79, Growth $149 [featured], Academy $249), no price shown on page
8. ProofSection — 2-col, left text + pull quote, right casual stacked screenshot collage
9. CustomSection — full-width surface-muted band, centered, tag cloud of custom options
10. FinalCTASection — --accent full-bleed green background, "Your school deserves software built by someone who understands the work."
11. Footer — 4 cols

**Motion**: framer-motion whileInView (once: true), entrance y: 16-20 → 0 + fade, stagger groups

## Required Output

Provide a detailed implementation plan including:

1. **File structure** — exact paths for all new files
2. **Package installs** needed (framer-motion version?)
3. **globals.css** — complete @theme block with all design tokens mapped to Tailwind utility names
4. **layout.tsx** — font setup approach with next/font/google
5. **Component architecture** — which components are 'use client' vs server, why
6. **Each section** — key Tailwind classes, layout approach, tricky implementation details
7. **ProductPreviewSection mockups** — specific HTML/CSS structure for each of the 7 tabs (kanban, form, checklist, etc.)
8. **Placeholder product visual** — for hero section and other product frames
9. **Animations** — specific framer-motion patterns to use per section
10. **Mobile responsive** — key breakpoint behaviors
11. **Font variable naming** — how Tailwind v4 `@theme` font variables connect to next/font CSS variables

Be very specific about Tailwind class names, especially for Tailwind v4 where custom tokens become utilities. Flag any Tailwind v4 specifics that differ from v3.

<!-- BEGIN:nextjs-agent-rules -->
# This is NOT the Next.js you know

This version has breaking changes — APIs, conventions, and file structure may all differ from your training data. Read the relevant guide in `node_modules/next/dist/docs/` before writing any code. Heed deprecation notices.
<!-- END:nextjs-agent-rules -->

1	# Eden-Inspired UI/UX Playbook for Next.js AI Agents
2	
3	This document defines a complete UI/UX system for AI agents building pages in a Next.js application. It is designed to capture the same broad design qualities seen in Eden’s public site: calm premium minimalism, editorial-product hybrid typography, soft neutral surfaces, product-led storytelling, and restrained motion.[cite:18][web:8]
4	
5	The user’s broader product context includes modern SaaS-style software and public-facing product marketing sites, so this system is written to support both a marketing website and authenticated product surfaces inside a single design language.[cite:18]
6	
7	## Core Design Intent
8	
9	The target aesthetic is not generic “AI startup” design. The direction should feel thoughtful, calm, premium, and creative; it should combine the trust and clarity of a well-structured SaaS product with the taste and emotional restraint of an editorial brand experience.[web:8]
10	
11	Agents should optimize for these traits on every page:
12	
13	- Calm, not loud.
14	- Premium, not flashy.
15	- Human and editorial, not overly corporate.
16	- Product-led, not decoration-led.
17	- Clean and confident, not crowded and over-explained.
18	
19	A page succeeds when a user can understand the primary action quickly, feel visual trust immediately, and sense that the product is polished before reading every detail.[web:8]
20	
21	## Brand Personality
22	
23	All pages should express a consistent personality:
24	
25	- Organized.
26	- Intelligent.
27	- Warmly minimal.
28	- Creative but controlled.
29	- Useful before impressive.
30	- Premium without luxury gimmicks.
31	
32	Use visual taste to signal quality. Avoid trying to signal innovation through glowing gradients, floating abstract blobs, or exaggerated 3D visual effects; those cues conflict with the Eden-inspired reference direction.[web:8]
33	
34	## Experience Principles
35	
36	### Narrative first
37	
38	Marketing pages should explain the problem emotionally, then show the product, then prove the workflow, then invite action. Eden’s homepage copy structure follows this kind of progression with conceptual statements, product framing, capability sections, and call-to-action transitions.[web:8]
39	
40	Agents should build pages in this order when possible:
41	
42	1. State the core problem or aspiration.
43	2. Show the product or interface quickly.
44	3. Explain the workflow in practical terms.
45	4. Reinforce trust with outcomes, examples, or use cases.
46	5. Present one clear next action.
47	
48	### Product as artwork
49	
50	Screenshots, dashboards, workspace mockups, and realistic interfaces should do more visual work than decorative graphics. Eden’s visible product imagery centers app-shell structures, content cards, sidebars, and search-led workspace panels, which makes the product itself the hero asset.[web:8]
51	
52	When agents need a hero visual, prefer one of these in order:
53	
54	- Real product screenshot.
55	- High-fidelity product mockup.
56	- Cropped workflow sequence.
57	- Only then, an abstract supporting visual.
58	
59	### One primary action
60	
61	Each section should have one dominant action and one supporting action at most. If an agent creates multiple equally emphasized buttons in the same viewport, the page will lose clarity and feel more generic.
62	
63	### Calm momentum
64	
65	Pages should feel alive through gentle reveal, layering, and microinteractions, but never through excessive animation. Motion should suggest polish and confidence, not attention-seeking behavior.
66	
67	## Visual Language
68	
69	### Overall look
70	
71	The UI should feel like a blend of:
72	
73	- Editorial software brand.
74	- Creative operating system.
75	- Minimalist productivity tool.
76	- Premium but understated SaaS.
77	
78	The visual system should rely on neutral backgrounds, layered surfaces, careful typography, and believable product depth rather than bright brand saturation.[web:8]
79	
80	### Stylistic summary
81	
82	Agents should consistently produce these qualities:
83	
84	- Warm off-white or soft stone page backgrounds.
85	- White or near-white card surfaces.
86	- Low-contrast borders.
87	- Spacious marketing sections.
88	- Dense but tidy product mockups.
89	- Rounded geometry with soft shadows.
90	- Muted accent color used sparingly.
91	- Serif or editorial display moments balanced with a modern sans-serif UI font.
92	
93	## Color System
94	
95	### Palette goals
96	
97	The palette should communicate calm sophistication. Most of the interface should be neutral. Accent color should guide action, not dominate the entire viewport.
98	
99	### Default palette
100	
101	Use this as the default Eden-inspired palette unless the product brand has its own established accent.
102	
103	```css
104	:root {
105	  --bg: #f6f5f2;
106	  --bg-alt: #f1efe9;
107	  --surface: #ffffff;
108	  --surface-soft: #fbfaf7;
109	  --surface-muted: #f3f1ec;
110	  --border: #e7e3dc;
111	  --border-strong: #d9d4cc;
112	  --text: #1f1e1a;
113	  --text-muted: #6f6b65;
114	  --text-faint: #9a958d;
115	  --accent: #244b46;
116	  --accent-hover: #1d3d39;
117	  --accent-soft: #dfe8e5;
118	  --success: #3f6b3f;
119	  --warning: #8a5a24;
120	  --error: #8b3752;
121	}
122	```
123	
124	### Color rules
125	
126	Agents must follow these rules:
127	
128	- Keep 80–90% of the page neutral.
129	- Use only one primary accent on any single page unless a chart or status system requires more.
130	- Reserve strong accent fills for buttons, active states, selected tabs, badges, and tiny emphasis moments.
131	- Use muted surfaces and subtle contrast steps to create depth.
132	- Never use blue-purple gradient branding by default; it breaks the desired aesthetic.
133	- Avoid pure black backgrounds and harsh white-on-black contrast unless a specific brand direction demands it.
134	
135	### Dark mode
136	
137	If dark mode is included, do not invert blindly. Use dark warm-charcoal surfaces, softened text contrast, and slightly lifted muted panels.
138	
139	Recommended dark variables:
140	
141	```css
142	[data-theme="dark"] {
143	  --bg: #171614;
144	  --bg-alt: #1c1b18;
145	  --surface: #201f1c;
146	  --surface-soft: #25241f;
147	  --surface-muted: #2b2925;
148	  --border: #34312c;
149	  --border-strong: #45413b;
150	  --text: #ece7df;
151	  --text-muted: #b4aea6;
152	  --text-faint: #8f887f;
153	  --accent: #7fa59e;
154	  --accent-hover: #95b8b1;
155	  --accent-soft: #273330;
156	}
157	```
158	
159	## Typography System
160	
161	### Font strategy
162	
163	The design language should use one clean sans-serif UI font and optionally one editorial display font for hero moments. The goal is “creative software with taste,” which is similar to the Eden-style breakdown previously established.[cite:18][web:8]
164	
165	Recommended combinations:
166	
167	- `Instrument Serif` + `Inter`.
168	- `Boska` + `Satoshi`.
169	- `Zodiak` + `General Sans`.
170	- `Source Serif 4` + `Inter` for a slightly more restrained version.
171	
172	### Recommended default
173	
174	Use this default pairing if no brand direction exists:
175	
176	- Display: `Instrument Serif`, serif.
177	- Body/UI: `Inter`, sans-serif.
178	
179	### Type scale
180	
181	```css
182	:root {
183	  --text-xs: 0.75rem;
184	  --text-sm: 0.875rem;
185	  --text-base: 1rem;
186	  --text-md: 1.125rem;
187	  --text-lg: 1.5rem;
188	  --text-xl: 2rem;
189	  --text-2xl: 3rem;
190	  --text-hero: clamp(3rem, 6vw, 5.5rem);
191	}
192	```
193	
194	### Type roles
195	
196	Agents should map typography like this:
197	
198	- Hero title: display font, `--text-2xl` or `--text-hero`, tight line-height, slight negative letter-spacing.
199	- Page title: display font or semibold sans, `--text-xl`.
200	- Section title: semibold sans or display font depending on tone, `--text-lg` to `--text-xl`.
201	- Body copy: sans, `--text-base` or `--text-md`.
202	- Small UI text: sans, `--text-sm`.
203	- Metadata and chip labels: sans, `--text-xs` or `--text-sm`.
204	
205	### Typography rules
206	
207	- Use serif only for large editorial moments, not for form labels or dense UI.
208	- Default all UI chrome to sans-serif.
209	- Keep body text between 60ch and 72ch max width.
210	- Use short paragraphs on marketing pages.
211	- Use stronger hierarchy through size and spacing, not by introducing too many font weights.
212	- Avoid more than five distinct text styles on a single page.
213	
214	### Heading style
215	
216	Hero and major section headings should feel concise, emotional, and precise. Avoid generic startup copy such as “Unlock the power of AI” or “Empower your workflow.” Eden’s homepage framing leans more reflective and benefit-oriented than hype-oriented, and that tone should guide future page copy patterns.[web:8]
217	
218	## Spacing and Layout
219	
220	### Base spacing scale
221	
222	Use a 4px-derived spacing system.
223	
224	```css
225	:root {
226	  --space-1: 4px;
227	  --space-2: 8px;
228	  --space-3: 12px;
229	  --space-4: 16px;
230	  --space-5: 20px;
231	  --space-6: 24px;
232	  --space-8: 32px;
233	  --space-10: 40px;
234	  --space-12: 48px;
235	  --space-16: 64px;
236	  --space-20: 80px;
237	  --space-24: 96px;
238	  --space-32: 128px;
239	}
240	```
241	
242	### Layout principles
243	
244	Agents should treat layout as a balance between roomy storytelling and compact product detail.
245	
246	Rules:
247	
248	- Marketing sections: generous vertical spacing.
249	- Product sections: tighter internal density within larger page padding.
250	- Text blocks: narrow enough to read comfortably.
251	- Feature grids: avoid identical three-card rows by default.
252	- Important sections: mix 1-column, 2-column, and asymmetrical compositions.
253	
254	### Container widths
255	
256	Recommended widths:
257	
258	- Narrow reading content: 680px max.
259	- Standard content container: 1200px max.
260	- Wide product showcase: 1280–1440px max.
261	- Full-bleed sections: use sparingly for hero visuals or screenshots.
262	
263	### Section rhythm
264	
265	Agents should vary vertical rhythm intentionally:
266	
267	- Hero: 120–160px top/bottom desktop.
268	- Standard sections: 88–120px desktop.
269	- Compact sections: 56–72px.
270	- Mobile: reduce proportionally but keep enough breathing room.
271	
272	Do not make every section use the exact same padding. That creates a templated feel.
273	
274	## Surface System
275	
276	### Surface philosophy
277	
278	Depth should come from layered neutrals, not heavy borders or dramatic shadows. Eden-like product visuals rely on calm container hierarchy and believable panel separation.[web:8]
279	
280	### Surface tiers
281	
282	Define at least four layers:
283	
284	- Page background.
285	- Alternate page background or section tint.
286	- Primary card surface.
287	- Soft inset or nested panel surface.
288	
289	### Surface usage
290	
291	- Use `surface` for cards, modals, and major panels.
292	- Use `surface-soft` for grouped UI blocks and inner nesting.
293	- Use `surface-muted` for page bands or subtle contrast sections.
294	- Pair with thin warm-gray borders.
295	
296	### Border and shadow rules
297	
298	- Use 1px borders by default.
299	- Border color should be quiet and low-contrast.
300	- Use soft, wide shadows with low opacity.
301	- Avoid thick borders, harsh outlines, and bright glows.
302	
303	Recommended shadows:
304	
305	```css
306	:root {
307	  --shadow-xs: 0 1px 2px rgba(20, 20, 20, 0.04);
308	  --shadow-sm: 0 6px 24px rgba(20, 20, 20, 0.05);
309	  --shadow-md: 0 14px 40px rgba(20, 20, 20, 0.08);
310	  --shadow-lg: 0 24px 60px rgba(20, 20, 20, 0.10);
311	}
312	```
313	
314	## Radius and Shape
315	
316	Rounded geometry is central to the desired look, but it should remain refined rather than bubbly.
317	
318	Recommended radius system:
319	
320	```css
321	:root {
322	  --radius-sm: 10px;
323	  --radius-md: 14px;
324	  --radius-lg: 20px;
325	  --radius-xl: 28px;
326	  --radius-pill: 999px;
327	}
328	```
329	
330	Rules:
331	
332	- Buttons: `--radius-pill` or `--radius-md` depending on brand tone.
333	- Cards: `--radius-lg`.
334	- Large product shells or modal surfaces: `--radius-xl`.
335	- Small chips or tag pills: `--radius-pill`.
336	- Avoid mixing too many radius values on one page.
337	
338	## Component Styling Guidance
339	
340	### Header
341	
342	The header should be minimal, quiet, and sticky or semi-sticky when appropriate.
343	
344	Guidelines:
345	
346	- Transparent or lightly frosted only if contrast remains excellent.
347	- Logo left, navigation center or right, CTA on right.
348	- Use small text sizing and enough horizontal breathing room.
349	- Avoid giant nav bars or oversized buttons.
350	- On scroll, slightly increase surface opacity and shadow to create depth.
351	
352	### Hero section
353	
354	A hero in this system should contain:
355	
356	- A concise emotional headline.
357	- A short explanatory paragraph.
358	- One primary CTA and optional secondary CTA.
359	- A real product mockup or workspace visual.
360	- Spacious layout with strong visual restraint.
361	
362	Hero rules:
363	
364	- Do not center everything by default.
365	- Left-aligned heroes usually feel more premium and grounded.
366	- Keep CTA count low.
367	- Use the product visual as the main spectacle.
368	
369	### Buttons
370	
371	Button design should be soft, tactile, and clean.
372	
373	Variants:
374	
375	- Primary: accent background, light text.
376	- Secondary: neutral surface, border, dark text.
377	- Ghost: no heavy fill, darker text on hover-tinted background.
378	- Text link: underlined or subtle arrow treatment.
379	
380	Button rules:
381	
382	- Height: 40–48px typical.
383	- Padding: 14–20px horizontal.
384	- Font: small sans, medium weight.
385	- Motion: quick but smooth hover lift or shade shift.
386	- Never use bright gradient fills by default.
387	
388	### Cards
389	
390	Cards should feel like product-quality containers, not generic CMS boxes.
391	
392	Card recipe:
393	
394	- White or slightly warm surface.
395	- 1px low-contrast border.
396	- Radius 16–24px.
397	- Soft shadow or none, depending on density.
398	- Comfortable padding.
399	- Clear title, muted supporting text, optional action or visual.
400	
401	Card rules:
402	
403	- Prefer asymmetrical card grids over perfect repetition.
404	- Make at least one featured card larger than the others when possible.
405	- Avoid icon-in-colored-circle patterns unless they are truly necessary.
406	
407	### Inputs and forms
408	
409	Forms should feel calm and trustworthy.
410	
411	Input styling:
412	
413	- Height: 44–52px.
414	- Radius: 12–14px.
415	- Border: subtle neutral.
416	- Background: white or slightly tinted surface.
417	- Placeholder: muted but readable.
418	- Focus: accent ring with soft halo, not neon.
419	
420	Form UX rules:
421	
422	- Use clear labels above fields.
423	- Use grouped spacing and strong visual rhythm.
424	- Break long forms into steps where possible.
425	- Show inline validation, never only global error messages.
426	
427	### Sidebars and app shells
428	
429	Because Eden-like visuals use app-shell compositions, authenticated app surfaces should use a clean shell pattern.[web:8]
430	
431	App shell rules:
432	
433	- Left sidebar with clear grouping.
434	- Search or command bar near top.
435	- Main content area divided into cards or panels.
436	- Quiet active state using tinted neutral or soft accent background.
437	- Icons simple and monochrome.
438	
439	### Tables and data blocks
440	
441	Data should feel integrated into the product language, not pasted from an admin template.
442	
443	Rules:
444	
445	- Use larger row padding.
446	- Alternate row tint only subtly.
447	- Avoid dense black borders.
448	- Use muted small caps or semibold labels for column headers.
449	- Add filters/search above the table in a soft utility bar.
450	
451	### Empty states
452	
453	Empty states should feel designed, not forgotten.
454	
455	Include:
456	
457	- A human headline.
458	- One explanatory sentence.
459	- A clear next action.
460	- Optional lightweight illustration or product-themed visual.
461	
462	## Motion System
463	
464	### Motion philosophy
465	
466	Animation should create calm momentum. The page should feel responsive and refined, but never energetic in a way that distracts from understanding.
467	
468	### Default timing
469	
470	```css
471	:root {
472	  --ease-standard: cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1);
473	  --dur-fast: 180ms;
474	  --dur-med: 280ms;
475	  --dur-slow: 480ms;
476	}
477	```
478	
479	### Motion behaviors
480	
481	Use these by default:
482	
483	- Fade-in with 12–24px upward translation on entrance.
484	- Stagger groups by 60–100ms.
485	- Card hover lift by 2–6px.
486	- Button hover darken/lighten with subtle translateY.
487	- Sticky header background transition on scroll.
488	- Screenshot parallax kept minimal.
489	
490	### Motion rules
491	
492	- Do not animate everything on load.
493	- Do not use aggressive spring motion for business/product pages.
494	- Use motion to support clarity, hierarchy, and responsiveness.
495	- Respect `prefers-reduced-motion`.
496	- Avoid infinite looping decorative animations except very subtle ambient effects.
497	
498	### Suggested Framer Motion patterns for Next.js
499	
500	- `whileInView` for section reveals.
501	- `viewport={{ once: true, amount: 0.2 }}` for marketing content.
502	- Shared layout transitions for tabs and shell state.
503	- Motion values for tiny hover transforms only.
504	
505	## Grid and Composition Patterns
506	
507	### Approved patterns
508	
509	Agents should favor these patterns:
510	
511	- Editorial split hero: text left, product visual right.
512	- Offset screenshot section: large image with supporting text block overlapping edge.
513	- Two-up story blocks alternating image/text.
514	- Uneven feature grid with one feature card spanning two columns.
515	- Testimonial or proof strips with dense, low-noise formatting.
516	- Persona/use-case cards in a restrained grid.
517	
518	### Patterns to avoid
519	
520	Avoid these defaults because they create the generic AI-template feeling:
521	
522	- Perfect three-column icon card row with identical card heights.
523	- Large centered hero with random gradient blob behind it.
524	- Overuse of floating glassmorphism panels.
525	- Purple-blue neon gradient branding.
526	- Over-decorated dashboards with colored icon bubbles.
527	- Feature sections with all text centered.
528	
529	## Page-Level Guidance
530	
531	### Marketing homepage
532	
533	Recommended structure:
534	
535	1. Header.
536	2. Hero with product visual.
537	3. Problem or aspiration framing.
538	4. Product workflow or capabilities section.
539	5. Feature group with varied card sizes.
540	6. Screenshots or use-case panels.
541	7. Trust/proof section.
542	8. CTA close.
543	
544	### Product feature page
545	
546	Recommended structure:
547	
548	1. Quiet hero.
549	2. One workflow diagram or screenshot.
550	3. Benefit explanation in plain language.
551	4. Supporting features.
552	5. Example use cases.
553	6. CTA.
554	
555	### Dashboard page
556	
557	Recommended structure:
558	
559	- Persistent sidebar.
560	- Top utility bar with search and contextual actions.
561	- Overview metrics in restrained cards.
562	- Main work area with one dominant panel and 1–2 supporting panels.
563	- Surface nesting to show hierarchy.
564	
565	### Settings/billing/admin pages
566	
567	These should still feel designed.
568	
569	Rules:
570	
571	- Use section grouping and cards.
572	- Use concise headings and helper text.
573	- Keep actions local to each card when possible.
574	- Make destructive actions visually separated but not theatrical.
575	
576	## UX Writing Rules for Agents
577	
578	Copy style should match the visual system.
579	
580	Rules:
581	
582	- Be specific.
583	- Be calm.
584	- Avoid exaggerated startup language.
585	- Prefer concrete benefits over vague claims.
586	- Keep headings short.
587	- Keep supporting text readable and useful.
588	
589	Good examples:
590	
591	- “Keep enrollment, billing, and family communication in one place.”
592	- “See every lead, form, and payment without switching tools.”
593	- “Capture notes fast, then turn them into usable work.”
594	
595	Bad examples:
596	
597	- “Revolutionize your institution with next-gen AI synergy.”
598	- “Unlock unparalleled growth and transformation.”
599	- “The future of work starts here.”
600	
601	## Accessibility Rules
602	
603	All generated pages must be accessible.
604	
605	Agents must ensure:
606	
607	- Semantic HTML structure.
608	- One clear `h1` per page.
609	- Buttons and links have clear text.
610	- Contrast meets WCAG AA.
611	- Focus states are visible.
612	- Forms have labels and inline help when needed.
613	- Motion is reduced when user preference requests it.
614	- Hit areas are mobile-safe.
615	
616	## Responsive Rules
617	
618	### Mobile principles
619	
620	The mobile experience should feel intentional, not compressed.
621	
622	Rules:
623	
624	- Stack content vertically earlier rather than forcing cramped desktop layouts.
625	- Reduce hero text, but preserve hierarchy.
626	- Keep buttons easy to tap.
627	- Collapse nav cleanly.
628	- Allow product mockups to crop elegantly rather than shrink into illegibility.
629	
630	### Breakpoint guidance
631	
632	Suggested breakpoints:
633	
634	- Mobile: 0–767px.
635	- Tablet: 768–1023px.
636	- Desktop: 1024px+.
637	- Wide desktop: 1440px+.
638	
639	### Mobile-specific UX
640	
641	- Prioritize one CTA near the top.
642	- Turn complex comparison grids into stacked cards.
643	- Keep cards visually separated with spacing and layering.
644	- Ensure sticky UI does not consume too much vertical space.
645	
646	## Next.js Implementation Guidance
647	
648	### Architecture
649	
650	Agents working in Next.js should separate design concerns into reusable primitives.
651	
652	Recommended layers:
653	
654	- `app/(marketing)/...` for public pages.
655	- `app/(app)/...` for authenticated product pages.
656	- `components/ui/` for primitives.
657	- `components/marketing/` for storytelling sections.
658	- `components/app-shell/` for dashboard/navigation patterns.
659	- `lib/design-tokens.ts` or CSS variables in global styles.
660	
661	### Primitive components to define once
662	
663	Agents should create and reuse these primitives:
664	
665	- `Container`
666	- `Section`
667	- `Button`
668	- `Badge`
669	- `Card`
670	- `Surface`
671	- `Input`
672	- `Textarea`
673	- `Select`
674	- `Modal`
675	- `PageHeader`
676	- `FeatureCard`
677	- `ProductFrame`
678	- `SidebarNav`
679	- `EmptyState`
680	
681	### Styling strategy
682	
683	If using Tailwind, map all important values to theme tokens so agents do not improvise per page.
684	
685	Recommended token categories:
686	
687	- Colors.
688	- Radius.
689	- Shadow.
690	- Font family.
691	- Font size.
692	- Spacing.
693	- Max widths.
694	- Animation durations and easing.
695

716	      soft: '0 6px 24px rgba(20,20,20,0.05)',
717	      elevated: '0 14px 40px rgba(20,20,20,0.08)',
718	    },
719	  },
720	}
721	```
722	
723	## Agent Decision Rules
724	
725	When an AI agent creates a new page, it should evaluate decisions in this order:
726	
727	1. What is the single primary user goal on this page?
728	2. Is this page primarily narrative, functional, or hybrid?
729	3. What is the most important visual proof element?
730	4. Which layout best supports clarity without becoming generic?
731	5. Where should the accent color appear to guide attention?
732	6. What can be removed to make the page calmer?
733	7. Is the result more product-led than decoration-led?
734	
735	### If the page is marketing-heavy
736	
737	Prefer:
738	
739	- More whitespace.
740	- Stronger heading hierarchy.
741	- Bigger product visuals.
742	- Fewer UI controls.
743	- More emotional narrative.
744	
745	### If the page is product-heavy
746	
747	Prefer:
748	
749	- Denser but clean layout.
750	- Strong surface hierarchy.
751	- Subtle utility controls.
752	- Faster interaction feedback.
753	- Minimal decorative copy.
754	
755	## Anti-Patterns
756	
757	Agents must avoid the following:
758	
759	- Blue/purple startup gradients as the main brand language.
760	- Generic AI hero copy.
761	- Symmetrical three-card feature rows by default.
762	- Overusing centered text.
763	- Heavy glassmorphism.
764	- Giant shadows and glow effects.
765	- Decorative floating shapes unrelated to the product.
766	- Random icon circles as filler decoration.
767	- Too many CTA buttons competing in the same viewport.
768	- Tiny text in dense UI.
769	- Overly playful animation in trust-sensitive pages.
770	
771	## Quality Checklist for Every Page
772	
773	Before finishing a page, the agent should verify:
774	
775	- The page has one dominant goal.
776	- The first viewport communicates purpose clearly.
777	- The layout feels calm, premium, and intentional.
778	- Typography hierarchy is obvious.
779	- The accent color is restrained.
780	- Product visuals carry real weight.
781	- Cards and surfaces feel soft and refined.
782	- There are no generic AI-template patterns.
783	- Mobile layout still feels premium.
784	- Accessibility basics are satisfied.
785	- Motion supports clarity instead of competing with it.

1	# School Stack — Landing Page Spec (Revised)
2	
3	## UI/UX Masterclass Build Document
4	
5	This revised document keeps the Eden-inspired design system intact while strengthening the positioning around the most important strategic advantage in the School Stack story: this product was built inside a real microschool by an operator who lived the exact operational pain it solves.[file:22][file:24]
6	
7	The original landing-page spec already had strong visual judgment, a clear interaction model, and an excellent product-preview concept. The biggest opportunity was not visual, but narrative: the founder story should move earlier and carry more of the page’s persuasion, because the business analysis identifies founder credibility and microschool-native authenticity as one of the company’s strongest differentiators.[file:23][file:24]
8	
9	---
10	
11	## High-Level Evaluation of the Existing Spec
12	
13	The design direction is strong. The page already captures the right visual tone: calm, editorial, product-led, and clearly influenced by the Eden-style system rather than a generic startup template.[file:22][file:23]
14	
15	What is working especially well:
16	
17	- The visual restraint is excellent, especially the warm neutrals, serif/sans pairing, rounded product frame, and low-noise section rhythm.[file:22][file:23]
18	- The interactive preview is the right “wow” moment because it makes the product itself the hero rather than relying on decorative marketing devices.[file:22][file:23]
19	- The copy tone is already much better than average SaaS landing pages; it is calm, specific, and operator-friendly.[file:23]
20	- The Sage Field proof section is directionally right, because it introduces the strongest credibility asset available to School Stack: real-world origin and use.[file:23][file:24]
21	
22	What should change:
23	
24	- The founder/operator story needs to appear earlier, before the full product-tour section, so visitors understand *why* this product feels more credible than generic school software.[file:24]
25	- The hero should frame School Stack less as “another all-in-one tool” and more as “software we built because the market failed us.” That framing is more ownable and more defensible.[file:24]
26	- The proof section should lean even harder into “built as one, not for one” and avoid any visual treatments that feel too SaaS-template-like, such as a thick accent left border on a quote block.[file:22][file:23]
27	- The page should reflect that the product is designed for the “too big for spreadsheets, too small for enterprise software” gap, because that exact category language from the market analysis is one of the sharpest positioning hooks available.[file:24]

31	## Core Narrative Shift
32	
33	The revised page should tell this story:
34	
35	1. Microschool founders are stuck between spreadsheets and bloated enterprise tools.[file:24]
36	2. School Stack exists because Sage Field ran into that exact problem in real operations.[file:24]
37	3. The product was built to solve those pains in a live school first, then turned into software other founders can use.[file:24]
38	4. The interactive preview proves the product is real and deep, not conceptual.[file:23][file:24]
39	5. The stack/pricing and custom sections show that buyers can start with structure and grow into tailored operations.[file:23][file:24]
40	
41	That sequence is stronger than leading with a broad all-in-one promise alone, because the business-grade document makes clear that School Stack’s biggest moat is authenticity, not abstract category language.[file:24]

45	## Design Principles for This Page

47	Pulled from the Eden-inspired playbook and sharpened for School Stack:
48	
49	- **Calm, not loud.** School founders are already overloaded. The page should feel like relief and operational clarity.[file:22][file:23]
50	- **Founder story before feature sprawl.** The product matters more once the visitor knows it came from a real school.[file:24]
51	- **Product as artwork.** The product preview remains the hero visual system and the most persuasive proof element.[file:22][file:23]
52	- **One action per section.** Never let the page feel like a noisy sales funnel.[file:22][file:23]

54	- **Editorial moments.** Use `Instrument Serif` for hero and major section headlines, `Inter` for body and UI.[file:22][file:23]
55	- **No generic SaaS.** No purple gradients, no glowing blobs, no icon-in-colored-circle feature grids, no hype-heavy AI language.[file:22][file:23]

6	
7	## What We Are
8	
9	School Stack is a software company that builds and sells school management platforms to microschool and small private school owners. Every feature in the platform was built inside a real, operating microschool — Sage Field in Texas — and proven there before being offered to anyone else.
10	
11	The microschool market is one of the fastest-growing segments in American education: $2.7 billion in 2024, projected to reach $9.6 billion by 2033. Between 750,000 and 2.1 million students are currently enrolled in microschools nationwide. The software serving them is fragmented, outdated, or built for the wrong audience entirely.
12	
13	School Stack is the only platform in this market that covers the full operational journey — from the first time a parent sees your school's website, through their application, their signed contract, their first tuition payment, and into daily classroom operations — in a single unified system, built specifically for how microschools actually work.
14	
15	---
16	
17	## The Problem We Solve
18	
19	Microschool founders are brilliant operators who built something special. But they're running their schools across five to ten disconnected tools — Google Forms for enrollment, Venmo for payments, Gmail threads for parent communication, PDFs for contracts, Wix for a website, Excel for budget. Every tool is a context switch. Every handoff between tools is a chance for something to fall through.
20	
21	The software alternatives that exist are either built for large school districts (expensive, overwhelming, wrong assumptions), built for childcare and daycare (Brightwheel, Kangarootime), or too lightweight to run a real school operation (Spark, Omella). Nothing was designed for the microschool operator managing 15–80 students, running every department alone or with a tiny team, and needing software that works like a natural extension of how they already think about their school.
22	
23	That is the gap School Stack owns.
24

238	## Competitive Landscape
239	
240	### Who exists and why they fall short
241	
242	**SchoolCues** ($1/student/month, ~$75/mo min) is the price-value incumbent — broad modules, affordable, 10+ years in the market. But its billing is confusing and hard to navigate, the UI is clunky, there is no CRM or admissions pipeline, no teacher observation notes, no health profiles, no contract versioning, and no budget tracking. Customers love the support team but frequently cite the software itself as frustrating.
243	
244	**Spark School Software** ($34.99/mo) was built "by microschoolers for microschoolers" and has the right identity — but very little depth. No CRM, no health forms, no contracts, no application pipeline, no tour booking, no budget module, no behavioral notes. Works for schools that need just a basic gradebook and payment link.
245	
246	**Brightwheel** ($600M+ valuation) is the category giant in childcare/daycare — exceptional parent UX, strong brand, viral word-of-mouth. But it is a childcare product, not a school product. No academic SIS, no enrollment wizard, no learning profiles, no CRM, no contracts. The lesson to take from Brightwheel: exceptional parent UX drives viral adoption. That is a design principle built into School Stack from day one.
247	
248	**Omella** (free + $1.99/transaction) handles payments and forms for microschools at zero monthly cost — strong hook for new founders. But it is not a school management system. No parent portal, no teacher dashboard, no CRM, no applications pipeline. Founders who start with Omella outgrow it quickly. That is a direct acquisition opportunity for School Stack.
249	
250	**PowerSchool, MySchoolWorx, Kangarootime** are all built for the wrong audience — large districts, faith-based K-12, or childcare centers. Wrong scale, wrong assumptions, wrong price.
251	
252	### The gap nobody fills
253	
254	Every competitor fails at the same things: a unified CRM from first inquiry to enrolled student, a multi-step application wizard with health forms and waivers, contract versioning with digital signatures, student health and behavioral profiles, teacher observation notes with attachments, budget tracking, multi-type Stripe checkout, and real-time operational notifications.
255	
256	**School Stack already has all of these — in production, proven at a real school.**
257	
258	### Our positioning
259	
260	School Stack occupies the position no competitor holds: high microschool specificity combined with enterprise-grade operational depth. The question we ask every prospect is not "does your current software have a gradebook?" — it is "does your current software handle everything from the first inquiry to the signed contract to the first tuition payment in one unified system, with zero data re-entry?"
261	
262	---
263	
264	## Why It's Different
265	
266	**Built by a founder, for founders.** School Stack was not designed by a software company guessing at school needs. It was built by someone who ran Sage Field from 0 to 30 students using it. Every feature exists because a real microschool needed it. That is a differentiation that money cannot buy and cannot be copied.
267	
268	**The full customer journey in one system.** The public marketing site, tour booking, waitlist, application, contract, tuition, daily feed, and messaging are all one product. No other platform in this price range manages the experience from "stranger on the internet sees your school" through "enrolled student in the classroom" without leaving the system.
269	
270	**Operational depth at small school pricing.** Enterprise features — contract versioning with diff viewer, multi-type Stripe checkout with webhook automation, student health profiles, behavioral strategies, CRM with tags and call logs, budget module with bank sync — at $79–$249/month.
271	
272	**Concierge setup.** Every customer gets their instance configured with their school's name, domain, programs, and branding. This is not a DIY SaaS product where they figure it out themselves. It is a ready-to-use system, handed over.
273	
274	**Not a childcare product wearing a school costume.** Every assumption in the platform is built around academic microschools: multi-step enrollment, parent-partnership models, flexible programs, small teams, close relationships, and founders doing everything themselves.
275	
276	---
277	
278	## Business Model
279	
280	Customers pay a monthly or annual subscription for their Stack tier. Annual plans include two months free, which improves lifetime value and reduces seasonal churn.
281	
282	On top of the subscription, schools commission Custom Stack features — bespoke additions scoped, priced, and built specifically for their school. This is the highest-margin tier and the strongest competitive moat: a school that has custom workflows built for them is deeply embedded and unlikely to switch.
283	
284	The relationship is ongoing. As a school grows, they upgrade their Stack. As their needs get more specific, they commission more custom work. Concierge onboarding is included for the first 10 customers and available as a premium add-on beyond that.
285	
286	---
287	
288	## Go-to-Market Strategy
289	
290	### Phase 1 — Foundation (Months 1–3)
291	
292	- Finalize the Sage Field case study with specific metrics: enrollment applications processed, revenue collected, hours saved per week, parent satisfaction
293	- Launch the School Stack website with the interactive product demo (tabbed preview, founder video, feature comparison vs. SchoolCues and Spark)
294	- Form School Stack LLC (separate from Sage Field LLC)
295	- Apply to the National Microschooling Center preferred provider directory — Spark is already listed; this is table stakes
296	- Close the first 3 paying customers via personal demo before any paid advertising
297	
298	### Phase 2 — Community-Led Acquisition (Months 2–6)
299	
300	Microschool founders concentrate in specific, highly accessible communities. Zero-cost acquisition:
301	
302	- Facebook groups: Microschool Revolution, "Microschool Founders," state-specific homeschool groups (Texas alone has tens of thousands of members), NMC member community
303	- Approach: add value for weeks before ever mentioning the product. Answer operational questions. Share free resources. When someone posts about software pain, respond with a genuine answer and offer to show how you solved it at Sage Field.
304	- Lead magnets: free downloadable templates (enrollment agreement, liability waiver, parent handbook, health form) — all already built for Sage Field, branded School Stack, gated behind an email
305	
306	### Phase 3 — Content & SEO (Months 3–9)
307	
308	High-priority content targeting low-competition, high-intent keywords:
309	
310	- "How to start a microschool in Texas"
311	- "Best microschool software 2025–2026"
312	- "SchoolCues alternatives"
313	- "How to manage microschool enrollment"
314	- "Microschool parent communication software"
315	- "How to collect tuition for a microschool"
316	- "Microschool application form template"
317	
318	### Phase 4 — Partnerships (Months 4–12)
319	
320	- **KaiPod Catalyst** — trains new microschool founders; be the recommended software at graduation
321	- **Microschool Revolution** — partner with their founder directory
322	- **National Microschooling Center** — preferred provider status + member pricing
323	- **Texas homeschool associations (THSC)** — sponsor events, exhibit at conferences, offer member pricing
324	- **Outschool, Prenda, and other microschool networks** — single partnership converts at scale
325	
326	### Phase 5 — Direct Outreach (Ongoing)
327	
328	The highest-converting channel for school software is direct, personal outreach from the founder. The story — "I built this for my own school, it worked, want to see it?" — closes faster than any ad.
329	
330	Outreach sequence:
331	
332	1. Find founders via Facebook groups, Instagram, NMC directory, Microschool Revolution listings
333	2. Engage with their content authentically for 1–2 weeks
334	3. DM or email: "I built the software for my own microschool, Sage Field — grew from 0 to 30 students with it. Happy to show you what it does in 15 minutes. No pitch, just a demo."
335	4. Demo → free trial → close
336	
337	Microschool founders are solo operators making fast decisions. Unlike K-12 districts (6–12 month sales cycles), a well-positioned microschool deal can close in days.
338	
339	---
340	
341	## 90-Day Launch Checklist
342	
343	- [ ] Form School Stack LLC in Texas (separate from Sage Field LLC)
344	- [ ] Secure schoolstack.com or equivalent domain
345	- [ ] Document Sage Field case study with specific metrics
346	- [ ] Build School Stack marketing website with interactive product demo and case study page
347	- [ ] Apply to NMC preferred provider directory
348	- [ ] Join 10+ microschool/homeschool Facebook groups; begin value-first engagement
349	- [ ] Publish first 3 SEO-targeted blog posts
350	- [ ] Create lead magnet package (enrollment agreement template + parent handbook + health form)
351	- [ ] Set up analytics (PostHog or equivalent) on the School Stack site
352	- [ ] Finalize pricing tiers and build Stripe billing for School Stack subscriptions
353	- [ ] Finalize annual plan pricing (2 months free) and 14-day free trial setup
354	- [ ] Reach out to 20 microschool founders with personal demo offer
355	- [ ] Close first 3 paying customers before any paid advertising
356	- [ ] Attend or present at one Texas homeschool/alternative education event
357	- [ ] Reach out to KaiPod Catalyst about partnership
358	
359	---
360	
361	## Proven at Sage Field
362	
363	School Stack is not a pitch deck. It is running right now at Sage Field Microschool in Texas — managing real enrollment applications, real families, real payments, real teacher assignments, and real school operations. The platform has been through the full enrollment cycle, handled Stripe payments, managed waitlists, sent emails, tracked leads, and powered a parent and teacher portal used by real people.
364	
365	That's the foundation every new customer is buying — not a prototype, not a beta, but a working system that was pressure-tested before it was ever sold.
366	
367	---
368	
369	## The Thesis
370	
371	The microschool software market has a clear gap: there is no platform that provides operational completeness — from marketing and lead capture through enrollment, contracts, billing, parent communication, and admin operations — at a price and with a UX that small school founders can actually adopt.
372	
373	SchoolCues is cheap but shallow. Spark is microschool-native but limited. Brightwheel is excellent but built for childcare. Omella handles payments but nothing else.
374	
375	School Stack has already built what does not yet exist in this market. The go-to-market strategy is not about building — it is about distribution, community, and story. The founder-led story, the Sage Field proof case, the depth of the platform, and the specificity of the target market create a competitive position that well-funded competitors cannot easily replicate.
376	
377	The goal is to be the first name every microschool founder hears when they ask "what software should I use to run my school?" — and the last one they ever need to switch away from.

content.text:
Now I have everything I need. Let me build the comprehensive implementation plan.

---

# School Stack Landing Page — Complete Implementation Plan

---

## 1. Package Installs

```bash
npm install framer-motion@12.38.0
```

framer-motion v12 is the current stable release with full React 19 / Next.js 16 compatibility. It ships ESM-first, which pairs cleanly with Next.js's bundler module resolution (`"moduleResolution": "bundler"` in your tsconfig). No additional type packages needed — types are bundled.

**Important Tailwind v4 note**: Tailwind v4 uses `@tailwindcss/postcss` as its PostCSS plugin, which is already in your devDependencies. No `tailwind.config.js` is created. All custom tokens live inside an `@theme` block in `globals.css`.

---

## 2. File Structure

```
src/
├── app/
│   ├── globals.css                          ← complete @theme block + base styles
│   ├── layout.tsx                           ← font setup, metadata, body classes
│   └── page.tsx                             ← composes all sections in order
│
├── components/
│   ├── ui/                                  ← atomic primitives
│   │   ├── Button.tsx                       ← primary / secondary / ghost variants
│   │   ├── Badge.tsx                        ← eyebrow chips
│   │   ├── Container.tsx                    ← max-width wrapper
│   │   └── FadeInView.tsx                   ← reusable framer-motion entrance wrapper
│   │
│   └── sections/                            ← one file per section
│       ├── Navbar.tsx                       ← 'use client' (scroll state, drawer state)
│       ├── HeroSection.tsx                  ← 'use client' (entrance animation)
│       ├── PainSection.tsx                  ← server (static content, CSS-only scroll reveal via FadeInView inside)
│       ├── FounderStoryBridgeSection.tsx    ← server
│       ├── ProductPreviewSection.tsx        ← 'use client' (tab state)
│       ├── WorkflowSection.tsx              ← server
│       ├── StacksSection.tsx                ← server
│       ├── ProofSection.tsx                 ← server
│       ├── CustomSection.tsx                ← server
│       ├── FinalCTASection.tsx              ← server
│       └── Footer.tsx                       ← server
│
└── lib/
    └── motion.ts                            ← shared framer-motion variants
```

**Why this structure**: The design spec references `components/marketing/` as the preferred folder. Using `components/sections/` is equivalent and cleaner for a single-page build. `components/ui/` holds the reusable primitives. `lib/motion.ts` centralises animation variants so they are consistent across all sections.

---

## 3. globals.css — Complete @theme Block

Tailwind v4 maps `@theme` tokens directly to utility classes. The naming rule is critical: `--color-{name}` becomes `bg-{name}`, `text-{name}`, `border-{name}`. The dashes in names like `surface-soft` map to `bg-surface-soft`. Custom properties that are NOT under `--color-*` do not auto-generate utilities — they become CSS variables only (accessible via `var(--name)`).

```css
@import "tailwindcss";

@theme {
  /* ─── Colors ─────────────────────────────────────────── */
  --color-bg:            #f6f5f2;
  --color-bg-alt:        #f1efe9;
  --color-surface:       #ffffff;
  --color-surface-soft:  #fbfaf7;
  --color-surface-muted: #f3f1ec;
  --color-border:        #e7e3dc;
  --color-border-strong: #d9d4cc;
  --color-text:          #1f1e1a;
  --color-text-muted:    #6f6b65;
  --color-text-faint:    #9a958d;
  --color-accent:        #244b46;
  --color-accent-hover:  #1d3d39;
  --color-accent-soft:   #dfe8e5;

  /* ─── Radius ─────────────────────────────────────────── */
  --radius-sm:   10px;
  --radius-md:   14px;
  --radius-lg:   20px;
  --radius-xl:   28px;
  --radius-pill: 9999px;

  /* ─── Shadows ─────────────────────────────────────────── */
  --shadow-xs: 0 1px 2px rgba(20,20,20,0.04);
  --shadow-sm: 0 6px 24px rgba(20,20,20,0.05);
  --shadow-md: 0 14px 40px rgba(20,20,20,0.08);
  --shadow-lg: 0 24px 60px rgba(20,20,20,0.10);

  /* ─── Typography ─────────────────────────────────────── */
  /* These connect to next/font CSS variables — see layout.tsx */
  --font-display: var(--font-instrument-serif), "Instrument Serif", serif;
  --font-body:    var(--font-inter), "Inter", sans-serif;

  /* ─── Easing (CSS vars only, not utilities) ─────────── */
  --ease-standard: cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1);
  --dur-fast:  180ms;
  --dur-med:   280ms;
  --dur-slow:  480ms;
}

/* ─── Base ──────────────────────────────────────────────── */
html {
  scroll-behavior: smooth;
}

body {
  background-color: var(--color-bg);
  color: var(--color-text);
  font-family: var(--font-body);
  -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;
  -moz-osx-font-smoothing: grayscale;
  text-rendering: optimizeLegibility;
}

h1, h2, h3, .font-display {
  font-family: var(--font-display);
  letter-spacing: -0.03em;
  line-height: 0.98;
}

@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) {
  *, *::before, *::after {
    animation-duration: 0.01ms !important;
    transition-duration: 0.01ms !important;
  }
}
```

**Tailwind v4 critical notes**:
- In v4, `--color-*` tokens auto-generate the full color utility family (`bg-*`, `text-*`, `border-*`, `ring-*`, `fill-*`, etc.). You do NOT need to write `bg-[var(--color-accent)]` — just write `bg-accent`.
- `--radius-*` → `rounded-sm`, `rounded-md`, `rounded-lg`, `rounded-xl`, `rounded-pill`.
- `--shadow-*` → `shadow-xs`, `shadow-sm`, `shadow-md`, `shadow-lg`.
- `--font-display` and `--font-body` → `font-display`, `font-body` utilities (applied as `font-family`).
- There is NO `--color-` prefix needed in class names. `bg-surface-soft` just works.

---

## 4. layout.tsx — Font Setup

`Instrument Serif` is NOT a variable font — it has fixed weights (400, 400 italic). `Inter` IS a variable font. The correct next/font approach:

```tsx
import { Inter, Instrument_Serif } from 'next/font/google'

const inter = Inter({
  subsets: ['latin'],
  display: 'swap',
  variable: '--font-inter',
})

const instrumentSerif = Instrument_Serif({
  subsets: ['latin'],
  weight: ['400'],
  style: ['normal', 'italic'],
  display: 'swap',
  variable: '--font-instrument-serif',
})
```

In the layout body:
```tsx
<html lang="en" className={`${inter.variable} ${instrumentSerif.variable}`}>
  <body className="font-body antialiased">
    {children}
  </body>
</html>
```

**How the font chain works**: `next/font` injects `--font-inter` and `--font-instrument-serif` as CSS custom properties on the `html` element. In `globals.css`, the `@theme` block defines `--font-display: var(--font-instrument-serif), ...` which Tailwind v4 picks up and exposes as `font-display`. When you write `font-display` on an element, it resolves through the chain: Tailwind utility → `--font-display` CSS var → `--font-instrument-serif` CSS var → the actual font stack. This is the correct v4 pattern — no `tailwind.config.js` override needed.

**Instrument Serif note**: This font is `Instrument_Serif` (underscore) in `next/font/google` imports, not `Instrument Serif`.

---

## 5. lib/motion.ts — Shared Animation Variants

```typescript
import type { Variants } from 'framer-motion'

export const fadeUp: Variants = {
  hidden: { opacity: 0, y: 18 },
  visible: {
    opacity: 1,
    y: 0,
    transition: { duration: 0.55, ease: [0.16, 1, 0.3, 1] },
  },
}

export const fadeIn: Variants = {
  hidden: { opacity: 0 },
  visible: {
    opacity: 1,
    transition: { duration: 0.45, ease: [0.16, 1, 0.3, 1] },
  },
}

export const staggerContainer: Variants = {
  hidden: {},
  visible: {
    transition: { staggerChildren: 0.08, delayChildren: 0.05 },
  },
}

export const staggerContainerSlow: Variants = {
  hidden: {},
  visible: {
    transition: { staggerChildren: 0.12, delayChildren: 0.1 },
  },
}

export const scaleIn: Variants = {
  hidden: { opacity: 0, scale: 0.97 },
  visible: {
    opacity: 1,
    scale: 1,
    transition: { duration: 0.6, ease: [0.16, 1, 0.3, 1] },
  },
}

// Standard whileInView props — use spread: {...inViewProps}
export const inViewProps = {
  initial: 'hidden',
  whileInView: 'visible',
  viewport: { once: true, amount: 0.2 },
} as const
```

---

## 6. FadeInView.tsx — Reusable Entrance Wrapper

```tsx
'use client'
import { motion } from 'framer-motion'
import { fadeUp, inViewProps } from '@/lib/motion'

export function FadeInView({
  children,
  delay = 0,
  className,
}: {
  children: React.ReactNode
  delay?: number
  className?: string
}) {
  return (
    <motion.div
      {...inViewProps}
      variants={fadeUp}
      transition={{ delay }}
      className={className}
    >
      {children}
    </motion.div>
  )
}
```

---

## 7. Component Architecture — Server vs Client

| Component | Directive | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `Navbar` | `'use client'` | `useState` for scroll opacity and mobile drawer open/close |
| `HeroSection` | `'use client'` | `motion.div` with `useReducedMotion()` check, entrance animation on mount |
| `FadeInView` | `'use client'` | `motion.div` is a client component |
| `ProductPreviewSection` | `'use client'` | `useState` for active tab, `AnimatePresence` for cross-fade |
| `PainSection` | Server | Static HTML; uses `FadeInView` (client) as a child, which is fine |
| `FounderStoryBridgeSection` | Server | Static HTML; uses `FadeInView` |
| `WorkflowSection` | Server | Static content |
| `StacksSection` | Server | Static content |
| `ProofSection` | Server | Static content |
| `CustomSection` | Server | Static content |
| `FinalCTASection` | Server | Static content |
| `Footer` | Server | Static content |
| `Button` | Server | No interactivity beyond CSS hover |
| `Badge` | Server | Pure display |
| `Container` | Server | Layout wrapper only |

Server components are the default in Next.js App Router. Only components that require browser APIs, event handlers, or React hooks need `'use client'`. The key pattern: a server component CAN render a client component child — the client subtree takes over. So `PainSection` is a server component that renders `<FadeInView>` (client) children, which is the optimal pattern for reducing client-side JS.

---

## 8. Section-by-Section Implementation

### Section 1 — Navbar

**File**: `src/components/sections/Navbar.tsx`
**Directive**: `'use client'`

**Scroll behavior**: `useEffect` with a `window.addEventListener('scroll', ...)` listener. At 0px: `bg-transparent`. Past 40px: `bg-surface shadow-xs`. Transition: `transition-all duration-300`.

**Key classes**:
```
fixed top-0 left-0 right-0 z-50
px-6 md:px-10 lg:px-16
h-16 flex items-center justify-between
transition-all duration-300
```

Logo: `font-display text-[18px] text-text`

Nav links: `hidden md:flex gap-8` → each link: `text-sm text-text-muted hover:text-text transition-colors duration-200`

## 10. Mobile Responsive Key Behaviors

### Breakpoint system
- `sm`: 640px (not used heavily — skip straight to `md`)
- `md`: 768px — most grids switch from 1-col to multi-col
- `lg`: 1024px — hero splits, asymmetric layouts activate
- `xl`: 1280px — max container widths

### Hero: `< lg`
- Single column, visual below text
- H1 reduces via `clamp()` naturally
- CTA row stacks to `flex-col` at `< sm` if buttons are long
- Product frame: `aspect-[4/3]` not fixed height

### Navbar: `< md`
- Hide center nav links (`hidden md:flex`)
- Show hamburger `md:hidden`
- Drawer full-screen from right

### ProductPreview: All sizes
- Tab bar: `overflow-x-auto` with `flex-nowrap`
- Frame: `h-[280px]` mobile vs `h-[560px]` desktop
- Caption bar always visible (not hidden on mobile)
- Active tab scrolled into view via `ref.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth', inline: 'center' })`

### PainSection pain strip: `< md`
- `flex-nowrap overflow-x-auto` instead of `flex-wrap`
- Horizontal scrollable row with hidden scrollbar

### FounderStoryBridge collage: `< lg`
- Collage rendered as a simple grid of 2 cards instead of the layered absolute positioning
- `grid grid-cols-2 gap-3` for the two smaller cards
- Product frame above at full width

### StacksSection: `< md`
- Single column stack, featured card loses the `-mt-4` elevation (flat stack)
- Featured badge still shows

### ProofSection collage: `< lg`
- Stacked cards converted to a simple list of 2 cards in a column (no overlap/rotation)

### FinalCTA: All sizes
- H2 already uses `clamp()` so responsive by nature
- Padding: `py-24 md:py-32`

## 12. Motion Choreography Summary

| Section | Pattern | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| Navbar | CSS `transition-all` on scroll | JS scroll listener |
| Hero | `initial/animate` stagger (not whileInView) | On mount |
| Hero product frame | `scaleIn` variant, 320ms delay | On mount |
| PainSection | `whileInView staggerContainer` → H2, body, strip | Scroll enter |
| FounderStoryBridge | Left: `fadeUp whileInView`. Right: `fadeUp` 120ms delay | Scroll enter |
| WorkflowSection | `staggerContainer` over 3 cards, 120ms stagger | Scroll enter |
| StacksSection | `staggerContainer` over 3 cards, 100ms stagger | Scroll enter |
| ProofSection | Left: `fadeUp`. Right collage: `fadeUp` delayed | Scroll enter |
| CustomSection | H2+body first, then tags `staggerContainer` 40ms | Scroll enter |
| FinalCTASection | H2+body `fadeUp`, then CTA 80ms later | Scroll enter |
| ProductPreview tab switch | `AnimatePresence mode="wait"` opacity 0→1, 220ms | Tab click |
| Mobile drawer | `framer-motion x: '100%' → 0`, `ease-standard` | Hamburger click |

**All `whileInView`** use `viewport={{ once: true, amount: 0.2 }}` to prevent re-animation on scroll-back.

**`prefers-reduced-motion`**: The CSS block in globals.css handles this at the CSS level. For framer-motion, use `useReducedMotion()` in the two client components that run on mount (Navbar, HeroSection) and short-circuit the animation values when true.

## 13. Tailwind v4 Specifics vs v3 — Critical Differences

1. **No `tailwind.config.js`**: All token definitions live in `@theme {}` inside globals.css. No `extend.colors`, no `extend.borderRadius`, etc.

2. **`--color-*` naming is mandatory**: To get `bg-accent`, you MUST define `--color-accent`. Defining `--accent` alone does NOT create a Tailwind utility — it becomes a CSS variable only. This is the most common mistake when migrating.

3. **`@theme` vs `:root`**: Design tokens used ONLY as CSS variables (not needing Tailwind utilities) can stay in `:root`. Tokens that need to generate utilities MUST be in `@theme`. The shadows, easing values, and duration values should stay in `:root` because Tailwind v4 has its own shadow scale — don't fight it. Use `shadow-[var(--shadow-md)]` as an escape hatch, or redefine `--shadow-*` in `@theme` to override Tailwind's defaults.

4. **`--radius-pill: 9999px`** maps to `rounded-pill`. In v3 you'd write `rounded-full`. In v4, since you're defining your own radius token, `rounded-pill` is the correct class.

5. **`--font-display` and `--font-body`**: In `@theme`, these create `font-display` and `font-body` utility classes that apply `font-family`. This is correct v4 behavior — no need for `fontFamily` in a config object.

6. **Arbitrary values still work**: `text-[clamp(2rem,5vw,5.5rem)]`, `bg-[rgba(248,247,244,0.7)]`, `h-[540px]` all work identically to v3.

7. **No `bg-opacity-*` utilities**: Tailwind v4 uses `bg-accent/50` syntax (slash opacity) natively. Same as v3's modern syntax.

8. **`border-border`**: Since you defined `--color-border`, you get `border-border` automatically. You do NOT need `border-[var(--color-border)]`.

## 14. Implementation Sequence

1. Run `npm install framer-motion@12.38.0`
2. Write `src/app/globals.css` with complete `@theme` block
3. Write `src/app/layout.tsx` with font imports and CSS variables applied to `html`
4. Create `src/lib/motion.ts` with shared variants
5. Create `src/components/ui/` primitives: `FadeInView.tsx`, `Button.tsx`, `Badge.tsx`, `Container.tsx`
6. Build sections in page order — `Navbar` first (always visible), then top-to-bottom
7. Wire everything in `src/app/page.tsx`
8. Test mobile at 390px (iPhone), 768px (tablet), 1440px (wide desktop)
